Title: Appeals court: $2.5B for wall illegally diverted by Trump Trump illegally diverted $2.5B for wall, appeals court decides Appeals court: $2.5B for wall illegally diverted by Trump: The military construction funds are being used to finance the border wall through Arizona, California and New Mexico
Author: Fischer, Howard

Title: When does a Supreme Court judgment become effective?: For state court appeals, 25 days "after entry of judgment." For federal court appeals, "a formal mandate does not issue unless specifically directed."
Text:
The Supreme Court is a court, like any other. The only operative portion of a Supreme Court opinion comes with the conclusion on its final page. The authoring Justice or per curiam court writes that "[t]he judgment of the" lower court is "affirmed," "reversed," "vacated," or "the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion." After the Court announces its judgment, the opinion always ends with four critical words: "It is so ordered." Retired Chief Justice Burger even authored a book by this title. The Supreme Court's decisions do not immediately permeate the rule of law from sea to shining sea. These four words trigger a far more mundane process that is typical to all courts.
Under Supreme Court Rule 44, both parties have "25 days after entry of the judgment or decision" to file a petition for rehearing. In the event that the case is urgent, pursuant to Rule 45.2, the Court can direct the clerk "to issue the mandate in [the] case forthwith." For example, the Court took this action in Bush v. Gore. Indeed, the Court did so as well in Cooper v. Aaron, but its judgment was swiftly resisted in Little Rock. Alternatively, the prevailing party can file an application for the Court to issue the judgment forthwith. The petitioner took this course in Boumediene v. Bush.
Rule 45 is a bit complicated. It has three paragraphs:
1. All process of this Court issues in the name of the President of the United States.
2. In a case on review from a state court, the mandate issues 25 days after entry of the judgment, unless the Court or a Justice shortens or extends the time, or unless the parties stipulate that it issue sooner. The fling of a petition for rehearing stays the mandate until disposition of the petition, unless the Court orders otherwise. If the petition is denied, the mandate issues forthwith.
3. In a case on review from any court of the United States, as defined by 28 U. S. C. § 451, a formal mandate does not issue unless specially directed; instead, the Clerk of this Court will send the clerk of the lower court a copy of the opinion or order of this Court and a certified copy of the judgment. The certified copy of the judgment, prepared and signed by this Court's Clerk, will provide for costs if any are awarded. In all other respects, the provisions of paragraph 2 of this Rule apply.
Paragraph two concerns appeals from state courts. In such cases, "the mandate issues 25 days after entry of the judgment."That date can be modified if the Court or parties request an earlier date. But the mandate is stayed if any party requests a petition for rehearing. Such motions are rarely granted. But until the mandate is issued, the state court lacks jurisdiction to take any action.
Paragraph three concerns appeals from federal courts. In federal appeals, there is no formal mandate, unless the Court specifically directs that a mandate should issue. Eventually, the Clerk of Supreme Court can send the clerk of the lower court a copy of the judgment. But there is no fixed time frame in which the judgment must be sent. The final sentence of paragraph 3 states, "In all other respects, the provisions of paragraph 2 of this Rule apply." Does that sentence mean that the Clerk must send the judgment within 25 days? Does that sentence mean that the parties can request the Clerk to send the judgment sooner than 25 days? The rules do not answer either of these questions directly, but practice suggests that the answers are yes and yes.
The Supreme Court decided Trump v. Vance on June 9. This appeal came from the Second Circuit. Therefore, there is no requirement that a formal mandate must issue, in 25 days or ever. On July 15, the New York District Attorney filed an application for issuance of a copy of the opinion and certified copy of the judgment forthwith. President Trump's private counsel consented to this motion.
Vance did not ask for a copy of the "mandate." There is no formal mandate under Rule 45.3. Instead, Vance asked for "a copy of the opinion" and "a certified copy of the judgment" to be sent to the Second Circuit. Yet, Vance still relied on Rule 45.2. I think he needs to rely on Rule 45.2 to provide a method to request the issuance of the judgment before 25 days. (See the last sentence of Paragraph 3). Finally, this request was sent to Chief Justice Roberts, rather than Justice Ginsburg, who is the Circuit Justice of the Second Circuit. Two days later, on July 17, Chief Justice Roberts granted the request.
The House of Representatives also requested the in Trump v. Mazars judgment should be issued forthwith (see here and here). However, Trump's private counsel opposed that request. I don't see any urgent reason for the Chief to grant this request. The House lost the case.
A similar issue arose in the DACA litigation. Regents was decided on June 18, 2020. And the docket still does not reflect the transmission of a formal judgment to the Circuit Court. But twenty-five days have already elapsed. Once again, people should recognize that the Supreme Court's judgments are not self-executing. Soon enough, district courts can carry the Supreme Court's judgment into execution. One District Court in Maryland has already issued such an order.
(The introduction to this post is based on my article, The Irrepressible Myth of Cooper v. Aaron.)
Word count: 927
Author: Blackman, Josh

Title: 7 candidates vie for judgeships: 4 state Supreme Court positions on the ballot
Text:
New York's judicial system can sometimes be confusing, and perhaps no more than when there is a very active race for state Supreme Court, such as this year with seven candidates for four open judgeships.
Merge that with the contentious fight on Capitol Hill over the appointment of a new Supreme Court Justice, and even more confusion can take hold.
First, New York has its own state Supreme Court, which has nothing to do with the federal Supreme Court, the ultimate arbiter of the nation's laws. The New York Supreme Court isn't even the highest level of courts in New York; that's the Court of Appeals.
There are also regional appellate courts, which are just below the Court of Appeals and review and decide whether lower court decisions and verdicts were legally correct, such as those that come from state Supreme Court.
Following so far?
Okay, then let's add another layer of possible confusion. The regional appellate courts actually consist of elected state Supreme Court justices, which are at some point in their judicial career appointed to the appellate divisions.
One such candidate, Stephen Lindley, happens to be a state Supreme Court justice who is on the appellate division and is seeking re-election this year.
Lindley is one of four Democrats on the ballot. He is hard to miss on the ballot, as he has been endorsed by both Democrats and Republicans — a very rare local cross-endorsement — as well as several third parties.
Asked about the unusual cross-endorsement, Lindley said, "I would like to think it's a reflection of the fair and even-handed manner that I've presided over cases in my judicial career and the nonpartisan way that I've conducted myself on the bench."
Also seeking re-election is Republican Daniel Doyle, who over the past 14 years has presided over the wide range of cases that make their way to state Supreme Court, with everything from criminal cases to medical malpractice to divorces.
"I've handled so many different things I've sort of become like a utility infielder for the district," Doyle said. "If a trial pops up at any given time, I can jump in and handle it."
Doyle, 57, and Lindley, 56, are seeking re-election to their second terms. Two other judgeships are open.
Also seeking one of the judgeships are two Republican County Court judges, Vincent Dinolfo and Samuel Valleriani; and Democrats Julie Cianca, a special assistant public defender; William Gargan, who heads the District Attorney's domestic violence bureau; and Gino Nitti, a former Greece town justice and former assistant district attorney who is now in private practice.
State Supreme Court justices serve 14 years and are paid $210,900.
The justices serve the eight-county Seventh Judicial District. The counties are Monroe, Cayuga, Livingston, Ontario, Seneca, Steuben, Wayne, and Yates.
Stephen Lindley
A former Rochester City Court judge, Lindley was appointed to the regional appellate court three years after his 2006 election to state Supreme Court.
If re-elected, Lindley would likely stay on the appellate court.
"I think my skill set is more appropriate to the appellate court than the trial court," Lindley said. Trial courts are more like umpires, he said, while the appellate judges decide whether a trial was fair and its outcome should stay intact or be reversed or altered.
With appellate panels of five, a judge must convince at least two others of the rightness of an opinion to carry a decision. "You have to collaborate and discuss cases with our colleagues and I've found that leads to better results," Lindley said.
During Lindley's tenure on the appellate division, there has been a noticeable shift in which the court has shown more willingness to seriously scrutinize judicial decision-making of the lower courts.
"I go where the facts and the law lead me," Lindley said. "I'm not afraid to reverse a case and dismiss an indictment if the evidence is insufficient and the law requires it.
"I am not afraid to modify a sentence as being harshly excessive," he said. "That used to be rarely, if ever, done."
With its published decisions and its hearings televised online, the appellate courts are the most transparent, Lindley said.
"We are the court you can follow if you are in Russia or somewhere," he said. "You can follow everything we do."
More information about Lindley can be found at facebook.com/judgelindey2020.
Daniel Doyle
Doyle admits the origin of his love for the law may sound corny to some, but, he said, a popular television show was key.
"I loved 'The Andy Griffith Show,' " he said. "The whole sense of how they portrayed justice through Sheriff Taylor impressed me as a kid, and even to this day."
In the show, Griffith's character was known to find a way to mete out justice with a humane and fair touch. "It really influenced in me this sense of right and wrong," Doyle said.
But more importantly was the late state Supreme Court Justice Harold Galloway, a family friend whose advice pointed Doyle toward a legal career. Known for his unbending ethical code, Galloway retired in 2011 and died in 2016.
"One of the greatest things about when I got elected (in 2006) was being able to serve at the same time he was serving," Doyle said.
Doyle had a career in private practice and with the Monroe County Law Department before his election. Last year, Doyle's peers in the Seventh Judicial District chose him as the 2019 Jurist of the Year.
Doyle said the experience of the past 14 years has helped him navigate the many types of matters that can land in state Supreme Court.
"When a case comes in, I can bring up a case I've handled that is similar," he said. "That's an added value in being able to move cases along, in being able to resolve cases short of trial if possible.
"Knowing the attorneys also helps me resolve cases, in my opinion."
More information about Doyle can be found at reelectjudgedoyle.com
Julie Cianca
A longtime public defender, Julie Cianca, 54, had not considered running for a judgeship until last year, when some local Democrats started putting her name out as a possible candidate. She then was registered in the Independence Party, avoiding politics, and had not envisioned herself on the bench.
"It was people telling me that they wanted somebody with my experience on the bench," she said of her decision to switch to the Democratic Party and run for state Supreme Court justice. "This wasn't so much me coming up with the idea.
"But with 25 years in the Public Defender's Office, you can live in a vacuum and you don't understand that there are people who truly appreciate the work you are doing."
Often, she said, people look to prosecutors for judgeships. But, many don't grasp the work of an individual representing the indigent, Cianca said.
"I've got people who are trusting me to know everything about their situation and advise them properly," she said. "If I make a mistake and advise somebody incorrectly or overlook something, a human being is going to pay with their freedom.
"I also reflect often on the basic humanity of the people that I do represent."
As well as her trial experience, Cianca has also been a supervisor in the Public Defender's Office, overseeing most of the office's different bureaus during the past 15 years.
In 2017, she was awarded the Jeffrey Jacobs Award for Trial Advocacy, an honor named after the late Jeffrey Jacobs who worked for the Public Defender's Office for 20 years before dying of cancer.
More about Cianca can be found at julieciancaforsupremecourt.com.
Vincent Dinolfo
A current County Court judge, Dinolfo, 62, decided this year, as his 10-year term expires, to instead seek election to state Supreme Court.
Dinolfo now oversees the county's diversion courts for addicts, the mentally ill, and veterans who may suffer from service-related substance abuse or mental illness. The courts try to route those individuals into treatment instead of jail or prison.
"We are kind of like the model treatment courts in the state and people come from all over the place to see how we do it here," Dinolfo said. The courts rely on accountability and honesty, said Dinfolfo, who said his courtroom motto is "Dignity in, dignity out."
"What I've found is about 99 percent of the people who get treated with dignity respond with dignity," he said.
The state's new bail reform laws have made it more difficult to direct people into the treatment courts because many are not incarcerated for long after the criminal allegations, Dinolfo said. But, he said, he and others have worked this year to find ways to get to defendants while in jail and try to convince them of the benefits of treatment if eligible.
"We're trying to come up with innovative ways of trying to reach them," he said.
Dinolfo acknowledged that a state Supreme Court race in a majority-GOP judicial district may be more hospitable to a Republican and Conservative-backed candidate than a race in the now-heavily Democratic Monroe County. But, he said, state Supreme Court is not foreign to him.
County Court judges can also serve as acting state Supreme Court justices, and Dinolfo has done so for much of his term.
"I've been an acting Supreme Court justice for most of the last 10 years," said Dinolfo, who was an Irondequoit justice before his election to Monroe County Court. "I've been on the bench for 21 years and I've loved every minute of being able to serve."
More about Dinolfo can be found at judgevincedinolfoforsupremecourt.com.
William Gargan
A longtime prosecutor whose first homicide case was in 2003, Gargan, 50, said his courtroom experience has laid a solid foundation for a judgeship.
As the head of the District Attorney's domestic violence bureau, Gargan said he is sensitive to the need for individuals to have their fears and concerns answered when in court, whether they be victims, witnesses, or the accused.
"I think I'm particularly attuned to ensuring that everybody that comes before the court ... has their voice heard," he said.
Prosecuting domestic violence cases often brings him in touch with some of the most vulnerable victims, Gargan said. It is a crime that continues, immune to downturns in other criminal activities, he said.
"Domestic violence is almost impervious. You can't put officers out and saturate an area."
Gargan, who joined the DA's Office fulltime in 1998, said a prosecutor must see all aspects of a case, and his year in the job would be an asset on the bench. "You recognize that to be a good prosecutor you work professionally and successfully with defense all the time," said Gargan, who ran unsuccessfully for City Court in 2014.
Gargan has been part of the office's leadership structure since 2006, when he became deputy chief of local courts. He also has prosecuted hate crimes, and spoken to prosecutors around the state about the statutes and how they can be enforced.
Gargan comes from a family of New York City police, including a grandfather who was fatally shot by a burglar in 1947.
More about Gargan can be found at votegargan.com.
Gino Nitti
A former prosecutor, Nitti, 59, also is no stranger to the bench; he spent 12 years as a Greece town justice before a falling-out with Republican leadership three years ago played a role in a decision to switch parties.
Nitti maintained that then-GOP Chairman Bill Reilich helped maneuver him from the Greece town justice ballot on the Republican line because of a petty political scuffle; Reilich claimed that Nitti did not seek the necessary petition signatures.
Nitti said that he thinks the Democratic Party is now more in sync with his beliefs and a need for an equitable justice system.
"People have lost confidence in our institutions and perhaps chief among them is our criminal justice system," he said.
In court, Nitti said, a judge can help redirect individuals who may find themselves in hard times or who may have made bad decisions that could upend their future.
"I've always had a passion for adjudication beyond just dealing with the intricacies and the nuances of the law, but having the opportunity to impact people's lives," he said.
Nitti worked for 12 years as a prosecutor, handling major felonies, and three years as a state parole commissioner. He also has worked as a deputy county attorney.
As a judge, Nitti said, there is "the balance of preserving the interest of the community at large while still recognizing that you're dealing with a human being."
More about Nitti can be found at ginonitti.com.
Samuel Valleriani
Valleriani, 46, has been an assistant public defender, a Gates town justice, a legal law clerk to a County Court judge, and he is now a County Court judge himself.
While those experiences have laid the groundwork for a state Supreme Court judgeship, Valleriani said, it was also his past employment as a city high school school law and government teacher that also gives him valuable insight.
"I think the diversity of my experience gives me a broader perspective," said Valleriani, who received the USA Today Experience Today Top Teacher of the Year Award in 2004. "Teaching is a real passion of mine."
He also had ample experience with the treatment courts beforehand as the legal clerk to former County Court Judge John DeMarco, who oversaw the treatment courts before an election loss last year.
"As his law clerk I saw him work tirelessly to continue the work of Judge (John) Schwartz," Valleriani said. Schwartz created the state's first Drug Treatment Court in the mid-1990s.
Elected to County Court in 2016, Valleriani has also had the role of acting state Supreme Court justice. He now is the "intake judge" for the felony-level treatment courts.
That position, he said, allows him to continue to work with the treatment courts as he did when DeMarco's clerk.
"That has also diversified my experience on the bench," he said.
A judgeship is "the purest practice of law because you're not taking a position," he said. "You're analyzing the facts and you're applying the law."
For more information on Valleriani, visit judgevalleriani.com.
Ratings, ballot lines
In judicial evaluations, the Greater Rochester Association for Women Attorneys, or GRAWA, gave Cianca and Gargan ratings of "well qualified." The GRAWA ratings are "exceptionally well qualified," "well qualified," "qualified," or "not qualified."
GRAWA recommended against other candidates because they declined to be interviewed. Some Republican candidates have boycotted the evaluations in recent years, claiming they are not fair.
The Rochester Black Bar Association rated Cianca and Gargan as "qualified." Its ratings are "highly qualified," "qualified," or "not qualified."
The Association also rates a candidate's "sensitivity" to people of color and disenfranchised people, and rated Cianca as "highly sensitive" and Gargan as "sensitive."
For voters, Lindley is the anomaly, with support from Democrats and Republicans. He also has the Conservative, Working Families, and Independence lines.
Also with the Conservative lines are Republicans Doyle, Dinolfo, and Valleriani. Also with the Working Families lines are Doyle, Valleriani, and Cianca.
Doyle, Valleriani, and Dinolfo also have the Independence lines.
Contact Gary Craig at gcraig@gannett.com or at 585-258-2479. Follow him on Twitter at gcraig1.

Author: Craig, Gary

Title: HOW DOES A CASE REACH THE SUPREME COURT?
Text:
As a notably long Election Day has now come and gone, the prediction of many experts that there may be legal challenges to multiple aspects of the vote have already come true. So what better time to review how a legal challenge to the election can make its way to the highest court in the land?
The first and most critical thing to understand about the functioning of the Supreme Court addresses what is really a common public misconception. Most voters are unaware that the Supreme Court does not have to accept any case. The idea that a case winds its way through the system and when it arrives at the Supreme Court they have to hear the case is inaccurate and completely impractical.
So, cutting right to the chase, it is theoretically and practically possible that any election-related case that rapidly climbs through the court system and ends up seeking review by the Supreme Court could end there, with a refusal by the court to hear the case.
Generally, a case can reach the Supreme Court in one of three ways:
1. On appeal from a federal circuit court
The most common way for a case to reach the Supreme Court is on appeal from a federal circuit court, which itself is a court of appeals. So one of the parties would be appealing the decision reached on appeal.
A party to a case who wants to appeal a decision of a federal circuit court files a petition to the Supreme Court for a writ of certiorari, or cert for short. Certiorari means to inform in Latin, which speaks to informing the Supreme Court that the party would like the circuit court's decision to be reviewed.
Here, the Supreme Court can decide to get involved by accepting the case or it can simply decline to hear the case, which would make the decision of the federal appellate court a final one not reviewable elsewhere.
While voting in this election is something that is carried out by the states, there are many federal regulations and laws that can be brought into play, including federal regulations on fair voting, voter suppression and an almost infinite number of angles that could be raised by claimants in a federal court.
2. On appeal from a state supreme court
A case can also make its way to the Supreme Court when one of the parties files an appeal from a state supreme court.
Each state has its own supreme court that is the final authority on state law. The vast majority of cases in any given state end there. Again, the Supreme Court is highly selective about any case it chooses to hear.
Could an election case come to the court from a state supreme court? Absolutely. In fact, this may be the most likely scenario.
State supreme courts would be the ultimate decision-makers in any vote-counting process scenario within the state. Imagine a case that challenges the propriety of vote-counting procedures and policies within a state begins to wind its way through state courts. Only in exigent or exceptional circumstances might the Supreme Court decide to review the decision of the state supreme court.
Again, it is absolutely worth noting that one possible way for the Supreme Court to ultimately decide a state voting issue would be not to take the case. The Supreme Court could reject the opportunity to review an ultimate decision of a state supreme court for a variety of reasons. The final result here would be that the decision of the state supreme court is the final law of the land and the state's election count, procedures or processes would not get a federal review.
3. Through the Supreme Court's original jurisdiction
While this is by far the least common way for a case to reach the Supreme Court, there are election-related scenarios that could come into play in the next days and weeks.
The concept of original jurisdiction means that rather than a case coming to the court via other courts (state supreme court or federal appellate court) the case begins at the Supreme Court.
Practically, what are the chances the Supreme Court would pass up a chance to hear an election-deciding case in 2020?
Don't fall into a trap here and think that because as of last week we now have a 6-3 conservative court that this means it is an activist court just waiting to get involved in deciding the selection.
In fact, the smart money might bet on this Supreme Court doing everything possible not to become embroiled in an election-deciding issue.
Why? Because it may have bigger fish to fry at a date in the not distant future. Just as ultimately making a decision in this election could define this court, it may choose to pass on involving itself in this process to retain its impact and focus for other issues important to the nation, including re-examining landmark cases that have become the law of the land over decades.
Remembering that the Supreme Court is theoretically independent and has discretion to decide which cases it will hear, it is not out of the realm of possibility for the justices to decline hearing an election-related case. If they do choose to hear a case, it will take a vote of only four of the nine justices to do so. Depending upon the exact nature of the case behind the writ of certiorari, the 6-3 conservative majority could prove a powerful aid or obstacle to getting those four required votes.
In a case where certiorari is declined, the decision of the lower court is final.
CREDIT: By Aron Solomon
Word count: 941
Author: Solomon, Aron

Title: Absentee, mail-in voting case ends in favor of state: Judge's hands tied by state Supreme Court
Text:
One legal battle over Tennessee voting access came to an end this week after a Davidson County judge ruled her hands are tied by a previous state Supreme Court order.
The case, a challenge by voting rights advocates to expand mail balloting to all voters in light of concerns over the spread of COVID-19, was one of the most high-profile cases over absentee ballots this year in Tennessee.
As a result of the high court's decision, Chancellor Ellen Hobbs Lyle dismissed all of the claims in the case, in favor of the state.
She said she no longer has the authority to rule on the case, and even if she did, by the time it could go to trial it would be moot, with the election just weeks away.
"Even in the time of a worldwide pandemic emergency, the Tennessee Supreme Court held that the Legislature's routine excuse-only absentee voting policy, enacted for normal times, nevertheless controls how persons exercise the right to vote during a pandemic, and stated, 'These policy choices will be judged by history and by the citizens of Tennessee. We, however, properly may not and will not judge the relative merits of them, regardless of our own views,'" she wrote in Tuesday order.
Case pushed for broad access
The case, one of a handful in the courts over the state's by-mail voting policies, pushed the state to confirm that people with special vulnerabilities to COVID-19 are eligible to vote by mail in both the August primary and the November general election.
The lawsuit was filed in Davidson County Chancery Court earlier this year by Memphis-area voting rights organization #UpTheVote901 and named residents asking the court to confirm COVID-19 concerns were a valid excuse for not voting in person.
Lyle granted a preliminary injunction in June, ruling the state needed to allow people worried about their risk of contracting COVID-19 to vote by mail, kicking off months of back and forth between her and the state over interpreting her order into the wording presented to voters.
In later arguments, the state's attorneys admitted those with underlying conditions are eligible to vote by mail under the "ill or hospitalized" excuse, which has since been spelled out clearly on absentee ballot application forms.
At one point, Lyle shamed the state in open court, threatening the attorneys and election officials with criminal contempt for their actions. The state has maintained they were trying to clarify the situation for voters, not attempting to flout her orders.
Then, in August, the Tennessee Supreme Court overturned her order.
In theory, the case would have continued in the lower court, but for the timing. The last day to request an absentee ballot is Oct. 27, just a week after Lyle issued her order, and an extremely short timeline to finish trial preparations.
"Election officials are busy assisting voters during this record early voting period. There is no time to savor this court victory," Secretary of State Tre Hargett said in an emailed statement.
Silver lining in loss
Although Tuesday's order in some ways means the plaintiffs lost their case, lead attorney Steven Mulroy said they still achieved most of their goals in filing the lawsuit.
"The practical effect was that almost anybody who really wanted to vote absentee could do so," he said. "While I would have preferred that we had been allowed to voluntarily dismiss after the November election, yesterday's setback, as a practical matter, isn't really that much of a setback.
"Obviously I'm biased, but I think it's a good thing for the electoral system, and the public's confidence in the electoral system, during this election and this pandemic."
Although the dismissal largely ends the case in Davidson County, the court will retain jurisdiction over it for 30 days, Mulroy said.
This means, should a question come up over the state's compliance with the state Supreme Court's order, or other issues stemming from the rest of the election period, the plaintiffs will be able to file motions in the same case to seek a legal remedy to the problem.
Tennesseans have cast at least 146,322 by-mail ballots statewide as of Monday evening, the Secretary of State's office reports, more than the number of absentee votes counted in 2016 and 2012 combined (64,323 in 2012; 64,199 in 2016).
A canvass of counties before early voting started showed most were seeing voters choose to vote by mail because they were over 60 years of age, a class already eligible before the pandemic. Early this year, state officials told counties to prepare as though all of those older voters would vote by mail, and it seems many of them are.
And they're likely to continue to do so, Mulroy predicts.
"Regardless of what the state decides to do or decides not to do, I think that the people of Tennessee are going to be using mail voting, a lot more than they had in years past," he said. "Although it will go down from the current high level, it won't go down all the way. The more electoral experience we have, with significant percentages of the populace voting by mail, the more likely it is over the long term Tennessee will finally come around, to what 34 other states have already done, which is a regime in which anybody who wants to can can vote this way."
Separate lawsuits over other elements of Tennessee election policy (signature matching, first-time voter ID, felony punishments for sharing application materials, deadline extensions) continue in federal court as Nov. 3 looms.
A spokesperson for the attorney general's office declined to comment when reached by email.
Reach reporter Mariah Timms at mtimms@tennessean.com or 615-259-8344 and on Twitter @MariahTimms.
More than 20 workers open mail-in ballots at the Election Commission so they can then be scanned in Nashville on Aug. 6. Tennesseans have cast at least 146,322 by-mail ballots statewide as of Monday.
Larry McCormack/The Tennessean
Word count: 980
Author: Timms, Mariah
